subject: ap-precalculus
title: Quadratic Modeling: Streaming Plays & Average Rate of Change
stimulus:
A musician released a new song on a streaming service. Several months later, the musician began using an app (at time $t = 0$) that counts the total number of plays for the song since its release. The table gives the total number of plays, in thousands, for selected times $t$ months after the musician began using the app:

| Months after musician began using the app | $0$ | $2$ | $4$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total plays since release (thousands) | $25$ | $30$ | $34$ |

The total number of plays, in thousands, can be modeled by the function $D$ given by $D(t) = at^2 + bt + c$, where $D(t)$ is the total number of plays, in thousands, and $t$ is the number of months after the musician began using the app.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the given data to write three equations that can be used to find the values for constants $a$, $b$, and $c$ in the expression for $D(t)$.

(ii) Find the values for $a$, $b$, and $c$ as decimal approximations.
parts.scoring_notes: Decimal approximations must be accurate to three decimal places. Second point earned with or without supporting work if correct values are identified. A response using 25,000, 30,000, and 34,000 (correct thousands translation) is eligible: $a = -125$, $b = 2750$, $c = 25{,}000$. Partial credit (1 for A1, 0 for A2) if two correct equations are present in a set of three AND one correct value.

scoring_points.description: Three equations involving $a$, $b$, and $c$ using the given input-output pairs: $c = 25$; $4a + 2b + c = 30$; $16a + 4b + c = 34$

alternatives.correct_example: With thousands translation: $c = 25000$, $4a + 2b + c = 30000$, $16a + 4b + c = 34000$ — eligible for full credit

wrong_examples: Using only two data points (two equations instead of three)
Writing $D(1) = at^2 + bt + c$ using $t = 1$ (not a data point in the table)
common_traps: Partial credit (1 for A1, 0 for A2) if exactly two correct equations appear in a set of three AND one correct value is identified
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point A1 is earned for presenting three equations involving $a$, $b$, and $c$ that use the given input-output pairs.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly substitutes all three data points into $D(t) = at^2 + bt + c$, generating three distinct equations in three unknowns.
Uses only two of the three data points; a system of three equations is needed to solve for three unknowns $a$, $b$, $c$.

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = -0.125$, $b = 2.75$ (or $b = \frac{11}{4}$), $c = 25$; may be presented as standalone values or in an expression for $D(t)$: $D(t) = -0.125t^2 + 2.75t + 25$
wrong_examples: $a = -0.12$ (decimal presentation error — first such error does not earn point)
common_traps: Point A2 may be earned with or without supporting work — if correct values appear, work is ignored
Thousands translation ($a = -125$, $b = 2750$, $c = 25000$) is also eligible for full credit
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point A2 is earned for correct values of $a$, $b$, and $c$ with or without supporting work. If correct values are identified, work should be ignored. Point A2 is earned for correct values presented as either standalone values OR in an expression for $D(t)$.

sample_responses.commentary: All three values are correct and expressed to at least three decimal places; the point is earned whether they appear as standalone values or embedded in the formula.
$a$ is reported to only two decimal places on the first decimal presentation error in the question, so the point is not earned.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the given data to find the average rate of change of the total number of plays for the song, in thousands per month, from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ months. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the average rate of change found in part B (i) to estimate the total number of plays for the song, in thousands, for $t = 1.5$ months. Show the work that leads to your answer.

(iii) Let $A_t$ represent the estimate of the total number of plays for the song, in thousands, using the average rate of change found in part B (i). For $A_{1.5}$ found in part B (ii), it can be shown that $A_{1.5} < D(1.5)$. Explain why, in general, $A_t < D(t)$ for all $t$ where $0 < t < 4$. Your explanation should include a reference to the graph of $D$ and its relationship to $A_t$.
parts.scoring_notes: Supporting work is required for (i) and (ii). Units not needed in (i). Final answer in (ii) may be reported as 28 thousand if supporting work shows the correct decimal approximation 28.375. If (i) has a decimal presentation error or is incorrect, the value from (i) can be used consistently in (ii) to earn the second point. Both the concavity/rate-of-change statement AND the secant line reference are required for the third point.

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change $\frac{D(4) - D(0)}{4 - 0} = \frac{34 - 25}{4} = 2.25$ thousand plays per month, in presence of a quotient using the given data values
common_traps: Supporting work is required — a quotient using the data values must appear
Units not needed in part (i) and are ignored if included
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point B1 is earned for a correct decimal approximation in the presence of a quotient with a difference that uses the given data values. In part B (i) units are not needed and are ignored if presented.

sample_responses.commentary: Shows the required quotient using the two given data points and delivers the correct decimal result $2.25$.
States the correct value but omits the supporting quotient; the computation connecting $D(4) - D(0)$ and the interval length $4$ must appear.

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate for $t = 1.5$: $y = 25 + 2.25 \cdot 1.5 = 28.375$ thousand plays (or equivalently $y = 34 + 2.25(1.5 - 4) = 28.375$), in presence of supporting work
wrong_examples: $y = D(1.5) = -0.125(1.5)^2 + 2.75(1.5) + 25 = 28.906$ (using the quadratic model instead of the linear estimate)
common_traps: Final answer may be reported as 28 thousand if supporting work shows the decimal 28.375
If part (i) has an error, the consistent value from (i) may be used in (ii) to earn the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point B2 requires supporting work. The final number in part B (ii) may be reported as 28 thousand provided the supporting work has a correct decimal approximation for the estimate. If a response in part B (i) is incorrect, the reported value in part B (i) as the average rate of change can be used to arrive at an estimate in part B (ii).

sample_responses.commentary: Uses the secant-line estimate from endpoint $(0, 25)$ with the average rate of change $2.25$, producing the correct estimate with supporting work.
Evaluates the quadratic model directly instead of using the linear estimate based on the average rate of change; the question specifically asks for the estimate from part (i).

scoring_points.description: Explanation must include (a) the graph of $D$ is concave down OR the rate of change of $D$ is decreasing on $0 < t < 4$, AND (b) reference to use of a secant line on $0 < t < 4$ OR a linear function with reference to $t$-values 0 and 4 that establishes the placement of the line below the graph

required_elements: graph of $D$ is concave down OR rate of change of $D$ is decreasing
secant line through $(0, 25)$ and $(4, 34)$ lies below the graph
alternatives.correct_example: The graph of $D$ is concave down on $0 < t < 4$. The estimate $A_t$ is the $y$-coordinate of a point on the secant line through $(0, D(0))$ and $(4, D(4))$. Because the graph of $D$ is concave down, this secant line lies below the graph of $D$, so $A_t < D(t)$ for all $t$ in $(0, 4)$.

wrong_examples: Stating only 'concave down' without referencing the secant line
Stating only 'the secant line is below the graph' without explaining why (concave down)
common_traps: BOTH components are required: (1) concavity/rate statement AND (2) secant line reference — either alone is insufficient for the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: To earn Point B3, the explanation must include: (1) the graph of $D$ is concave down OR the rate of change of $D$ is decreasing, AND (2) a reference to the use of a secant line on $0 < t < 4$ OR the use of a linear function with reference to $t$-values $0$ and $4$ that provide the placement of the line.

sample_responses.response: The estimate $A_t$ is the $y$-coordinate of a point on the secant line passing through $(0, D(0))$ and $(4, D(4))$. Because the graph of $D$ is concave down on $0 < t < 4$, this secant line lies below the graph of $D$ on that interval, so $A_t < D(t)$ for all $t$ in $(0, 4)$.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides both required elements: concavity of $D$ (element 1) and the secant line connecting the data endpoints lying below the curve (element 2).

sample_responses.response: The graph of $D$ is concave down on $0 < t < 4$, so $A_t < D(t)$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the concavity but does not reference the secant line or the linear estimate; both elements are required to earn the point.

parts.prompt: The quadratic function model $D$ has exactly one absolute minimum or one absolute maximum. That minimum or maximum can be used to determine a domain restriction for $D$. Based on the context of the problem, explain how that minimum or maximum can be used to determine a boundary for the domain of $D$.
parts.scoring_notes: A response focusing on the left endpoint is also eligible. The location of the absolute maximum at $t = 11$ is not required, but if included it must be correct. If the left-endpoint approach is used, the value $t \approx -6.916$ is not required, but if included it must be accurate to at least one decimal place.

scoring_points.description: Explanation must include one of the following: (a) The total number of plays cannot decrease AND the function model $D$ decreases after it achieves its absolute maximum (at $t = 11$); therefore the domain has right endpoint $t = 11$. OR (b) The total number of plays is nonnegative AND the left endpoint of the domain is the value of $t$ at which $D(t)$ changes from negative to positive (approximately $t = -6.916$)

required_elements: total number of plays cannot decrease
alternatives.correct_example: The total number of plays cannot decrease, and the quadratic model $D$ achieves its absolute maximum at $t = 11$ and decreases after that. Therefore the domain should have right endpoint $t = 11$.

required_elements: total number of plays is nonnegative
$D(t)$ changes from negative to positive at the left boundary
alternatives.correct_example: The total number of plays is nonnegative, so the domain begins at the value of $t$ where $D(t)$ changes from negative to positive (approximately $t \approx -6.916$).

wrong_examples: Stating the absolute maximum is at $t = 11.5$ or another incorrect value (if location is included, it must be $t = 11$)
Not connecting the maximum/minimum to the real-world constraint on plays
common_traps: If the absolute maximum location is stated, it must be $t = 11$ — incorrect location voids that part
If $t \approx -6.916$ is stated for the left-endpoint approach, it must be accurate to at least 1 decimal place
scoring_points.official_rubric: To earn Point C1, the explanation must include one of: (a) the total number of plays cannot decrease AND the function model $D$ decreases after the time it achieves its absolute maximum — the location at $t = 11$ is not required but must be correct if included; OR (b) the total number of plays is nonnegative AND the left endpoint of the domain is the only value of $t$ at which $D(t)$ changes from negative to positive — the value $t \approx -6.916$ is not required but if included must be accurate to at least one decimal place.

sample_responses.response: The total number of plays cannot decrease. The quadratic model $D$ achieves its absolute maximum at $t = 11$ and then decreases. Therefore the domain of $D$ should have right endpoint $t = 11$.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the real-world constraint (plays cannot decrease) to the model property (decreasing after the maximum), and correctly identifies the boundary at $t = 11$.

sample_responses.response: The total number of plays cannot be negative, so the domain ends where $D$ becomes negative.
sample_responses.commentary: This reasoning addresses the left boundary, not the right; the required connection is between plays not decreasing and $D$ decreasing after its maximum to establish the right endpoint.
